questions.solution_html: The Line containing point E is AE.

questions.question_html: How many lines can pass through, one given point?
questions.solution_html: There are Infinite numbers of lines can pass through a single point.

questions.question_html: How many lines can pass through, two given points?
questions.solution_html: A unique line passes through two given points.

questions.solution_html: Point P lies on AB

questions.solution_html: XY and PQ intersect at M

questions.question_html: Draw a rough figure and label suitably in the case: a line l contains E and F but not D.
questions.solution_html: Line contains EF but not D

questions.question_html: Draw a rough figure and label suitably in the cases: {tex}\overline{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} and {tex}\overline{\mathrm{OQ}}{/tex} meet at O.
questions.solution_html: OP and OQ meet at O.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\mathop {{\text{MN}}}\limits^ \leftrightarrow {/tex}.
Q, M, O, N, P are points on the line {tex}\mathop {{\text{MN}}}\limits^ \leftrightarrow {/tex}.

options.explanation:
True.
As from the figure, we can see the line is passing through all the points.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
M, O, N are points on a line segment {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
True.
We know that a line segment contains every point on the line between its endpoints. So, MN is a line segment with end points M and N & has O in between its endpoints.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
M and N are end points of line segment {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
True.
We know that a line segment contains every point on the line between its endpoints. Moreover, MN is a line segment with endpoints M and N.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
O and N are end points of line segment {tex}\overline{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False.
If OP is the line segment then O and N can’t be its endpoints as the name it self tells us that O and P are the endpoints.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
M is point on ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False.
A ray has only one end-point and it extends infinitely in the other direction. For example, sun rays.
So, the ray OP starts from O and extends itself in the direction of P and it doesn’t have M on it as we can see from the figure.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
Ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} is different from ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{QP}}{/tex}

options.explanation:
True.
Since Both the rays has different origin points O and Q.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
Ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}}{/tex} is not opposite to ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False.
Ray OM is Opposite to ray OP as can be seen in the figure.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
O is not an initial point of {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex}

options.explanation:
False.
O is the Initial point of OP, as the name itself suggests. it is starting from point O.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
M is one of the end points of line segment {tex}\overline{\mathrm{QO}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False.
M can’t be one of the endpoints of the line segment QO as its endpoints are Q and O as the name suggests.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
Ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OP}}{/tex} is same as ray {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OM}}{/tex}.

options.explanation:
False.
We can see both the rays are starting from the same point but extending in opposite directions, So they can’t be same.

questions.question_html:
Consider the figure of the line {tex}\overline{\mathrm{MN}}{/tex}.
N is the initial point of {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{NP}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{NM}}{/tex}

options.explanation:
True.
N is the initial Point of NP and NM and have opposite direction.

Consider the given figure and answer the question: Is it a curve?
questions.solution_html: Yes, it is a curve.

questions.question_html: Consider the given figure and answer the question: Is it closed?
questions.solution_html: Yes, it is closed curve.

questions.question_html: Illustrate, if possible, with a rough diagram: An open curve made up entirely of line segments.
questions.solution_html: It is an open curve made up entirely of line segments.

questions.question_html: Illustrate, if possible, with a rough diagram: A polygon with two sides.
questions.solution_html: A polygon with two sides isn’t possible. Because a polygon is a closed shape with minimum number of three sides.

questions.question_html: Illustrate, if possible, with a rough diagram: A closed curve that is not a polygon.
questions.solution_html: A polygon is a two-dimensional figure which has a minimum 3 or more straight lines. So, the circle isn’t a polygon.

questions.question_html: Name the angles in the given figure.
questions.solution_html: ∠A or ∠DAB; ∠B or ∠ABC; ∠C or ∠BCD; ∠D or ∠CDA.

questions.solution_html: Point B lies on {tex}\angle{/tex}EOF.

questions.question_html: In the given diagram, name the point in the interior of {tex}\angle{/tex}DOE
questions.solution_html: Clearly, Point A is in the interior of {tex}\angle{/tex}DOE.

questions.question_html: Draw a rough diagram of two angles such that they have one ray in common.
